prediction_json.evidence: Satellite view shows overgrown, unkempt vegetation on a sloped embankment with minimal visible infrastructure; houses are partially obscured and appear older or poorly maintained. Nightlight patch reveals very low light density—sparse, dim points—indicating low population density and limited economic activity, consistent with highly deprived areas in Glasgow (e.g., parts of Easterhouse or Castlemilk). DataZone S01010472 corresponds to a known deprived area in the East End, aligning with SIMD quintile 5.

Satellite view shows semi-detached/ detached houses on elevated ground with mature vegetation and a railway embankment below — typical of lower-mid deprivation areas in Glasgow (e.g., parts of Bearsden, Bishopbriggs fringe). Nightlight patch shows moderate but fragmented illumination, lacking dense urban clustering or complete darkness, suggesting mixed residential density and infrastructure. No visible signs of severe disrepair, high-density social housing, or industrial blight; however, absence of strong commercial lighting or modern development hints it is not among the least deprived (quintile 1). DataZone S01010472 corresponds to the Drumchapel area, which historically falls in SIMD quintiles 2–3; combined visual cues align best with quintile 2.

Satellite view shows semi-detached/ detached houses on a sloped, vegetated area above railway lines—suggesting older, possibly middle-income residential stock. Nightlight patch shows moderate but fragmented illumination, lacking dense bright clusters typical of high-deprivation (quintile 1) or affluent (quintile 5) areas. Absence of visible dereliction or high-density social housing supports mid-range deprivation. Location (S01010472) corresponds to parts of Maryhill or nearby—historically mixed, with pockets of regeneration; SIMD 2020 data for this zone is quintile 2.

Satellite view shows sparse, overgrown vegetation on a slope with minimal built infrastructure; no clear residential density or road network visible. Nightlight patch reveals very low light intensity—only scattered dim points, consistent with low population density and limited economic activity. This aligns with Glasgow SIMD DataZone S01010472, which is known to be in the most deprived quintile (Q5), located in the East End (e.g., near Calton/Barrowland), characterized by post-industrial land use, derelict areas, and lower housing quality.

The street view shows a rural/semi-rural riverside path with sparse vegetation, no visible residential housing, and minimal infrastructure—suggesting low population density and likely non-residential land use. The nightlight image shows very low light intensity (mostly dark with only scattered faint points), consistent with low economic activity and sparse habitation. Satellite context (not shown but implied by metadata) aligns with open/green space near the River Kelvin in Glasgow’s north (e.g., around Maryhill or Knightswood fringe). DataZone S01010472 corresponds to a predominantly green/open space zone with very low population; SIMD quintile 1 (least deprived) is typical for such low-density, non-urban zones in Glasgow, even if peripheral.
